coredata.dc:title: Hyperactivation of lipases by immobilization on superhydrophobic graphene quantum dots inorganic hybrid nanoflower
coredata.dc:creator: Mostafavi, Mostafa
Poor, Mahtab Beihaghi
Habibi, Zohreh
Mohammadi, Mehdi
Yousefi, Maryam
coredata.dc:description: Various nanoflowers are synthesized for enzyme immobilization. In order to increase the activity of nanoflowers, in this study, 3D flower-like structured organic–inorganic hybrid nanoflowers (hNFs) with various lipases Rhizomucor miehei lipase (RML), Candida antarctica lipase B (CALB), Humicola insolens lipase (HIL), Thermomyces lanuginosus lipase (TLL), Eversa® Transform 2.0 (ET) a genetically modified enzyme derived of TLL and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared and characterized.Lipase hNFs [lipase-(Cu/Co)3(PO4)2] and lipase@GQDs hNFs [lipase@GQDs-(Cu/Co)3(PO4)2] were straightforwardly prepared through mixing with metal ion (Cu2+or Co2+) aqueous solutions with or without GQDs. The ET@GQDs-(Cu)3(PO4)2 hNFs demonstrated 687 % higher activity than ET-(Cu)3(PO4)2 hNFs and 650 % higher activity than the free ET. Similar results were also observed with other lipase hybrid nanoflowers. For example, TLL@GQDs-(Cu)3(PO4)2 hNFs exhibited a 557 % higher activity than TLL-(Cu)3(PO4)2 hNFs and a 463 % higher activity than free TLL. Additionally, TLL@GQDs-(Co)3(PO4)2 hNFs showed a 141 % higher activity than TLL-(Co)3(PO4)2 hNFs and a 304 % higher activity than free TLL. Upon examining pH and thermal stability, it was revealed that lipase@GQDs hNFs exhibited higher activity compared to free lipase and other hNFs without GQDs. The effect of metal ions, enzyme concentrations and amount of GQDs on the morphology and enzyme activity of the lipase-hNFs was examined.

Characterization and evaluation of the synthesized nanoflowers
Storage stability and reusability of immobilized ET

The authors would like to express their gratitude to Dr. Jesper Brask (Novozymes) for his contributions to scientific discussions, assistance, and valuable comments.

Encapsulation yield and specific activity of various ET-hNFs with different enzyme concentrations (0.05, 0.1 and 0.2 mg mL−1). For comparison, specific activity of free ET was measured to 90 U mg−1. Activity recovery of ET-hNFs with 0.1 mg mL−1 of ET concentrations.

Encapsulation yield and specific activity of various enzyme-hNFs with different enzymes (HIL, RML, TLL and CALB). Specific activity of free HIL, RML, TLL and CALB was measured to be 613, 6.3, 22 and 35 U mg−1 respectively.

head.ce:title: Hyperactivation of lipases by immobilization on superhydrophobic graphene quantum dots inorganic hybrid nanoflower

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Various nanoflowers are synthesized for enzyme immobilization. In order to increase the activity of nanoflowers, in this study, 3D flower-like structured organic–inorganic hybrid nanoflowers (hNFs) with various lipases Rhizomucor miehei lipase (RML), Candida antarctica lipase B (CALB), Humicola insolens lipase (HIL), Thermomyces lanuginosus lipase (TLL), Eversa® Transform 2.0 (ET) a genetically modified enzyme derived of TLL and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared and characterized.Lipase hNFs [lipase-(Cu/Co)3(PO4)2] and lipase@GQDs hNFs [lipase@GQDs-(Cu/Co)3(PO4)2] were straightforwardly prepared through mixing with metal ion (Cu2+or Co2+) aqueous solutions with or without GQDs. The ET@GQDs-(Cu)3(PO4)2 hNFs demonstrated 687 % higher activity than ET-(Cu)3(PO4)2 hNFs and 650 % higher activity than the free ET. Similar results were also observed with other lipase hybrid nanoflowers. For example, TLL@GQDs-(Cu)3(PO4)2 hNFs exhibited a 557 % higher activity than TLL-(Cu)3(PO4)2 hNFs and a 463 % higher activity than free TLL. Additionally, TLL@GQDs-(Co)3(PO4)2 hNFs showed a 141 % higher activity than TLL-(Co)3(PO4)2 hNFs and a 304 % higher activity than free TLL. Upon examining pH and thermal stability, it was revealed that lipase@GQDs hNFs exhibited higher activity compared to free lipase and other hNFs without GQDs. The effect of metal ions, enzyme concentrations and amount of GQDs on the morphology and enzyme activity of the lipase-hNFs was examined.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data supporting the findings of this study are available upon request. Researchers interested in accessing the data may contact Corresponding authors at Z_habibi@sbu.ac.ir (Z. Habibi) or M.yousefi@avicenna.ac.ir (M. Yousefi) for further information.

ce:section.ce:para: Enzymes are macromolecules which had excellent catalyst activity of specific substrate under mild conditions [1]. Lipases (triacylglycerol ester hydrolase, EC 3.1.1.3) are a class of enzymes, which catalytically hydrolyze the ester bonds of lipids to free fatty acids and glycerol and/or its di- and monoacylglycerols derivatives. However, they can also catalyze the hydrolysis of a variety of other natural and none-natural esters as well as converting acids and alcohols to ester bonds [2].

Lipase have an interesting mechanism of action while facing hydrophobic environments which is called interfacial activation and has been studied in many lipase-catalyzed reactions such as biodiesel production [3–5], resolution of racemic compounds [6] and selective hydrolysis of fish oils [7]. In the presence of a hydrophilic surface, lipase molecules tend to cover their active center by a polypeptide chain (lid) in order to be isolated from the hydrophilic reaction medium (closed form). In the presence of a hydrophobic environment, the lid is opened to expose the active center thus, enabling lipases to catalyze reactions [8].

Application of enzymes in free form are limited because of their poor stability in different operational conditions (temperature, pH) and the difficulty of recovery of enzymes in their soluble forms from the reaction medium [9]. Making heterogeneous biocatalysts by the immobilization of enzymes is a promising approach to overcome these drawbacks and improve activity, stability, and also reusability of enzymes [10–12].

It has been reported that enzyme conformation and activity can be altered by applying different immobilization techniques such as physical adsorption, cross-linking, entrapment and covalent binding [10,13–16]. Some immobilization methods can have deleterious effect on enzyme activity, more likely due to slight distortions in the enzyme structure during immobilization process, mass-transfer limitation of immobilized enzymes, and application of harsh condition for immobilization [17,18].

Recently, nanomaterials such as nanoporous silica [19], nanofibers [20], magnetic nanoparticles [21], carbon nanotubes [22], polymer beads [23], nanoflowers [14,24,25] have been used to improve the catalytic properties and stability of immobilized enzymes [26]. Flower-like nanostructures (nanoflowers) is a novel organic and inorganic hybrid platform, [27–29] that schematically resemble plant flowers in a nanoscale range and has attracted a lot of attention over the last years as a carrier for immobilization of enzymes [30,31]. Zare and his co-workers firstly reported utilizing hybrid nanoflowers (hNFs) comprising copper phosphate (Cu3- (PO4)2) to prepare immobilized enzymes with highly enhanced activity and improved stability [14]. The high surface-to-volume ratio of hNFs leads to considerable enhancement in their loading capacity, efficient loading capability, and excellent catalytic ability. Furthermore, the synthesis of hybrid enzyme-inorganic nanoflowers is simple, green and facile strategy, providing relatively low energy strategy for enzyme immobilization [14,32–34].

The particular interactions of the enzyme molecules and metal ions has been reported to be another crucial reason for the enhanced activity of enzymes after immobilization on hNFs [14,35,36]. However, during crystal nucleation and growth, the enzyme molecules could be embedded in the crystals structure [37], so the activity and stability of resulting biocatalysts are reduced in some reported studies [38]. Recently, it has been proposed that the incorporation of surfactants [39], biopolymers such as chitosan [40], and carbon-based nanomaterials [41] could improve the catalytic properties and stability of enzyme-containing nanoflowers.

Besides, fixation of lipases hydrophobic supports via physical adsorption is a very simple technique to facilitate their one-step purification, stabilization, and overactivation [42,43], since this way, the open form of lipases become fixed on the support [44]. Therefore, attempts have been made to embed relatively hydrophobic materials such as carbon nanotube (CNT) and graphene oxide (GO) in the structure of nanoflowers to improve hydrophobic properties of this support and enhance the final activity of the immobilized lipase [45]. GO nanoparticles are micro meter-sized large sheets from several hundred nanometers to tens of micrometers. The large size of the sheets may fully cover the enzyme surface and prohibit the minimum conformational change of the enzyme that is required for its catalytic performance. It can also hinder the accessibility of substrate into the active center of the enzyme and reduce the enzyme activity [46]. Reducing the size of graphene sheets and number of hydrophilic groups in GO surface can be considered as an effective way to overcome this problem.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a type of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials with lateral size <10 nm, have received significant attention owing to their unique characteristics such as excellent water solubility, ease of preparation, low toxicity, better biocompatibility, excellent optical and chemical stabilities [47].

In the present work, we developed lipase hybrid nanoflowers with or without embedding quantum dots (QDs), called E@GQDs-(Cu/Co) and hNFs E-(Cu/Co), respectively. To date, no reports have been published on the preparation of enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers doped with quantum dots.

Eversa® Transform 2.0 (ET), RML, CALB and HIL were generous gifts from Novozymes A/S, Denmark. TLL, Copper (II) sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O), cobalt (II) chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O), NaCl, KCl, Na2HPO4, KH2PO4, K2HPO4, HCl, NaOH, citric acid, 4-nitrophenyl butyrate (p-NPB), Coomassie brilliant blue G250 and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich company. The substrate solutions and Bradford solutions were freshly prepared before each experiment. All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used without any further purification.

In this work, the GQDs were synthesized by directly pyrolyzing citric acid (Fig. 1 ) [48]. In a typical procedure, 2 g citric acid was heated to 200 °C for 5 min until citric acid became melted. The color of the liquid was first changed to pale yellow, and then orange in 30 min, showing the formation of GQDs. If the heating was kept on for another 2 h, the orange liquid would finally turn to black solid, suggesting the formation of GO. The resulted orange liquid was added dropwise into 100 mL of 10 mg mL−1 NaOH solution, under vigorous stirring. After adjusting the pH to 7.0 with NaOH, the aqueous solution of GQDs (2 % w/v) were obtained and stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of lipase-containing nanoflowers and lipase-GQDs nanoflowers
ce:section.ce:para: The lipases (TLL, RML, CALB, ET and HIL) hybrid nanoflowers (L-hNFs) or lipase-GQDs hybrid nanoflowers (L-GQDs-hNFs) were prepared according to Zare et al. [14] and Stamatis et al. [45] with some modifications. For the synthesis of ET hybrid nanoflowers, Eversa (0.1 mg mL−1) and different amount of GQDs (0, 0.5, 1, 2.5, 5, 10, 20 or 50 mg) were added to phosphate buffer saline (PBS 1×, pH 7.4, 15 mL). The mixture was gently shaken and incubated at room temperature to induce electrostatic attraction between the components [49]. Subsequently CuSO4 (120 mM, 150 μL) or CoCl2 (120 mM, 300 μL) was added, and the mixture was shaken and left to incubate for 3 days at 4 °C to form ET and ET-GQDs hNFs. After incubation, the precipitates of ET-(Cu/Co)3(PO4)2 and ET@GQDs-(Cu/Co)3(PO4)2 were separated by centrifugation (9000 rpm, 10 min) and washed three times with distilled water to remove unreacted components.

In a typical method to synthesis hNFs, CoCl2 (120 mM, 300 μL) or CuSO4 (120 mM, 150 μL) aqueous solutions were added to phosphate buffer saline (PBS 1×, pH 7.4, 15 mL) containing different concentrations of Eversa (0.05, 0.1 or 0.2 mg mL−1) or contained (0.1 mg mL−1) of other lipase (TLL, RML, CALB and HIL) with or without GQDs. Then the resulting mixture was shaken for a while and incubated at 4 °C for 3 days. Then the ET-(Cu/Co)3(PO4)2 or ET@GQDs-(Cu/Co)3(PO4)2 precipitates were separated by centrifugation (9000 rpm, 10 min) and washed three times with distilled water to remove unreacted components and then were dried at room temperature under vacuum and stored at 4 °C. To calculate encapsulation yields in the NFs, the enzyme concentration in the supernatant was measured with the Bradford method [50].

Hydrolytic activity of free lipase and lipase-hNFs (with or without GQDs) were determined by measuring the increase in absorbance at 348 nm (ε = 5150 M−1 cm−1) produced by the release of p-nitrophenol during the hydrolysis of p-nitrophenylbutyrate (dissolved in acetonitrile) in 25 mM sodium phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) at room temperature. For this, 20 μL lipase suspension (0.5 mg lipase hNFs in 2 mL phosphate buffer 25 mM, pH 7.5) was added to 2 mL of the reaction mixture (0.8 mM p-NPB in phosphate buffer). The hydrolysis rate was followed by monitoring the change in absorbance over 2 min [51]. The specific activity is given as 1 μmol of p-nitrophenol released per minute per mg of the enzyme (IU) under the conditions described above.

Activity recovery (AR) of immobilized ET with 0.1 mg mL−1 of ET concentration was determined by the following equation:
AR
%
= Activity of immobilized
ET
A i
/ Activity of soluble
ET
A F
× 100

1 mg of lipase-hNFs was incubated in a 2 mL solution containing 1 M of NaCl with magnetic stirring for 24 h. Then, the concentration of lipase in the supernatant was measured by the Bradford's method.

The effect of pH on the stability of the immobilized lipases and their soluble forms was evaluated by incubating the free lipase and 0.5 mg of the immobilized derivatives in 2 mL sodium phosphate buffer (25 mM, pH 5.5–9.5) at 4 °C for 24 h. Thermal stability of the immobilized lipases and their soluble forms was determined by incubating the soluble lipase and 0.5 mg of the immobilized derivatives in 2 mL phosphate buffer (25 mM, pH 7.0) at 30, 40, 50, 60, 70 and 80 °C for 2 h. The half-life of free and immobilized ET was examined at 80 °C. The residual activity of each ET-hNFs was calculated as the ratio between the activity after incubation and the activity before incubation. The residual activity of each biocatalyst was calculated as the ratio between the remaining activity after incubation and the initial activity. The remaining activity was measured according to the standard assay as described in Section 2.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and evaluation of the synthesized nanoflowers
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of the synthesized nanoflowers were evaluated by using Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FE-SEM) (SIGMA VP, ZEISS). The constituent elements of the nanoflowers were analyzed by an energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) instrument in conjunction with the SEM. For X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis (Powder diffractometer, STOE-STADV), the synthesized nanoflowers were washed with DI water and dried at 70 °C for 1 day. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of nanoflowers were recorded on a FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo Nicolet Nexus 470).The fluorescence luminescence spectra were recorded with the use of a Fluorescence spectrometer LS 45 Perkin Elmer equipped with a 2 cm quartz cell, and the slit widths were set at 10 nm and these spectra were recorded over a wavelength range of 300 to 550 nm.

The Michaelis Menten constant (Km) and the maximum reaction velocity (Vmax) of p-NPB hydrolysis by free and immobilized ET were evaluated in the enzyme assay solution with p-NPB (0.2–2 mM) at 25 °C in phosphate buffer 25 mM at pH 7.0. Time course of absorbance increase at 348 nm was monitored for the release of p-nitrophenol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Storage stability and reusability of immobilized ET
ce:section.ce:para: Storage stabilities of ET nanoflowers were evaluated by incubating in phosphate buffer (pH 7.0, 25 mM) at room temperature for 30 days and residual activity was measured as the percentage of the initial activity.

The reusability of ET-hNFs (2 mg) was also tested in repeated hydrolysis reaction of p-NPB under the reaction conditions (0.8 mM p-NPB in 12 mL phosphate buffer 25 mM, pH 7.0). At the end of each catalytic run, the biocatalysts were separated from the reaction mixture by centrifugation at 9000 rpm for 10 min, washed with phosphate buffer (25 mM, pH 7.0), and incubated again with fresh substrate for the next reaction cycle. This was carried out for five cycles of p-NPB hydrolysis. Residual activities were reported as enzyme activity in each cycle relative to the activity of the original immobilized enzyme (100 %).

For the results, data are presented as mean ± standard deviation. Statistical analysis was achieved using Microsoft Excel software by one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA), in which statistical significance was measured for a p < 0.05. A minimum of three experiments was considered for each test.

Fig. 1 shows a schematic illustration of the fabrication of GQDs from pyrolysis of citric acid The mechanism of hNF formation is described in three successive steps; nucleation, growth and completion (Fig. 2 ). The nucleation plays a crucial role for the formation of hNFs. In this step, divalent metal ions (M2+: Cu2+ or Co2+) initially react with phosphate groups to form metal phosphates, and subsequently, they coordinate with electron donor groups on the enzyme and GQDs to initiate the nucleation of nanocrystals. In the second step, the growth of primary crystals is propagated by combining lipase-M3(PO4)2 crystals to produce nano petals. The formation of hNF is eventually completed through the anisotropic growth in the final step. Proteins and metal ions count as key components to form complete hNFs. The protein acts as a “glue” in the growth stage to hold nano petals together for the formation of large agglomerates of protein [37,52].

Fig. 2 schematically shows the preparation procedures for the lipase-hNF in the first path (A). In path (B), lipase@GQDs-hNF was prepared which started with a simple shaking of GQDs and lipase molecules in an aqueous PBS solution [53], followed by the addition of CuSO4 or CoCl2 solution.

The effect of ET concentration and the amount of GQDs were investigated, and the optimal condition were used to immobilization of another lipases (HIL, RML, TLL and CALB). At first the effect of the GQDs concentration (0, 0.5, 1, 2.5, 5, 10, 20 and 50 mg mL−1) were investigated for ET (0.1 mg mL−1). The results shows that the best enzyme activity was achieved for 2.5 mg mL−1 of GQDs, so this amount was chosen as the optimum concentration of GQDs for other enzyme derivatives of nanoflowers (Table 1 ).

Various ET concentration (0.05, 0.1 and 0.2 mg mL−1) were investigated which it was found that the best ET concentration was 0.1 mg mL−1 (Table 2 ). These optimum values (2.5 mg and 0.1 mg mL−1 from GQDs and enzyme respectively) were used in preparation of nanoflowers with another lipase (HIL, RML, TLL and CALB).
The structural properties of the hNF derivatives were characterized by EDX analysis, SEM, XRD, element mapping, fluorescence and FT-IR spectroscopy.

The surface morphology of the nanoparticles was visualized with scanning electron microscope (SEM) images (Figs. 3 and S1), showing that the nanoflowers have been created from the linking several hundred nano petals to each other. The existence of GQDs, type of the enzyme, and metal ions directly alter the size and morphologies of hNFs, which consequently affect the functional properties and stability of the immobilized enzyme. Therefore, the effect of different metal ions such as Cu2+ and Co2+ on the hNF morphology was investigated. The results showed that Cu2+ and Co2+-mediated nanoflowers had an ordered flower like structure, whereas hNFs doped with GQDs did not show flower-like appearance, but rather being large amorphous aggregates with unorganized and scattered petals. Furthermore, the protein concentration is an important parameter that has a great influence on the morphology and surface composition of the hNFs. A long and thin plates and no flower-like structures were formed once there was no protein in the structure, indicating the role of “glue” played by protein [14]. However, the flower-like nanostructures were clearly observed only at lower concentration of enzyme. In the higher concentrations of enzyme, an aggregated structure were formed which was in accordance of previous reports [54].

As is demonstrated in Fig. 3, the flower-like morphology appeared in the lipase-immobilized cobalt and cupper phosphate without GQDs. Compact and ball like structures and thicker nanosheet aggregates were observed instead of nanoflowers while GQDs and lipase were simultaneously used with copper and cobalt phosphate. GQDs concentration effect like enzyme on the nanoflowers morphology which the flower-like nanostructures are clearly seen at lower concentration of enzyme whereas an increase in enzyme concentration typically caused formation of aggregated structures, as described in previous literatures [14]. In comparison, the flower petals of lipase@GQDs-(Cu/Co)3(PO4)2 were mostly covered by GQDs (Fig. 3c and d). GQDs like GO and CNTs have the ability to interact and coordinate with M2+ due to presence of oxygen on their surface [41]. The SEM images also proved that the different lipases produced not identical surface morphology, which is most likely duo to the different degree of coordination between M2+ with the protein [14]. Also type of lipase can be affected on the morphology of hNFs which when HIL were used as organic part, the morphology of hNFs was like African marigold and rose, respectively for Cu and Co metal ions and when CALB and cobalt were used, the morphology did not show flower-like appearance. In Fig. 4 the small size of GQDs can be seen which prove the existence of QDs in the structure of nanoflowers, while it is according to previous literature published by Velumani and coworkers [55].

The distribution of metals in the nanostructures was determined by scanning the samples using SEM-EDX and element mapping analyses were performed by the energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) (Figs. 5, 6
, S2 and S3). The results confirmed that the used metals (Cu or Co), carbon, nitrogen, phosphorous, and oxygen are homogeneously distributed inside the lipase-containing hNFs. The molar ratio of metal to phosphorous for all the structures was around 1.5 (3:2 M:P), which is in good agreement with a protein-M3(PO4)2.3H2O composition of the nanocrystals. Fig. 5 also revealed that, the percentage of carbon in lipase@GQDs-Cu/Co hNFs is more that observed in nanoflowers without GQDs, proving the successful doping of GQDs in the structure of nanoflowers.

The lipase nanoflowers were also characterized with X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis in the scanning range of 5°–80° as shown in Fig. S4. The XRD patterns of lipase-Cu3(PO4)2, lipase@GQDs-Cu3(PO4)2, lipase-Co3(PO4)2 and Lipase@GQDs-Co3(PO4)2 hNF were consistent with crystal structures of Cu3(PO4)2·3H2O (JCPDS card 22−0548) and Co3(PO4)2·3H2O (JCPDS card 41−0375).

In Fig. 7 , fluorescence emission spectrums are showed which GQDs nanoflower gives a peak at 410 nm upon excitation at 280 nm to prove that the GQDs are existed in nanoflower structures. Fig. 8 shows the emission of blue light for GQDs nanoflowers after excitation with UV beam (365 nm). In 2020, Park et al. proved that graphene quantum dots on ZnO nanostructures showed fluorescence emission [56].

FTIR spectroscopy was used to further approve the structure of the lipase metal phosphate hNFs (with or without GQDs) in comparison with the corresponding metal phosphates without incorporated enzyme. The FTIR spectrum in Fig. 9 revealed the characteristic peaks of both the protein (~1600 cm−1) and the phosphate group (~1000 cm−1). The pure metal phosphates in Fig. 9 shows strong characteristic absorptions around ~1050 cm−1 (asymmetric stretching), ~950 cm−1 (symmetric stretching), and ~650 cm−1 (bending), all of which can be attributed to PO bond vibrations. The typical vibrations for the protein molecules occurred at 1650 and 1500 cm−1 for the amide bond, 2800 to 3000 cm−1 for CH bonds of –CH2 and –CH3, and around 3300 cm−1 for the hydroxyl groups. The signals around 1120 and 904 cm−1 can be assigned to the CO bonds and epoxy groups (C-dots), respectively. [14].

To evaluate the effect of GQDs on the activity and stability of lipases, five different lipases together with two metal ions were used in the synthesis of hNfs. As can be seen in Table 1, the amount of GQDs had different effects on the encapsulation yield and activity with different metals. Increase in GQDs caused the decrease of enzyme encapsulation in both metal ions [Cu3(PO4)2 and Co3(PO4)2] hNFs. GQDs can replace enzymes in nanoflowers, which with increasing amount of QDs, this replacement increases to some extent that there is no more enzyme banded to the metal ions. These observations indicated that each metal phosphate has a limited capacity to bind to the organic part.

ET specific activity is directly affected by the type of metal ions and the amount of GQDs which used in hNFs synthesis. Table 1 represents that, without GQDs, ET- Cu3(PO4)2 hNFs activity decreases (69 U mg−1) compared with free ET (90 U mg−1) whereas ET- Co3(PO4)2 hNFs specific activity has 1.7 fold increase (151 U mg−1) in its activity compared with free ET (90 U mg−1). However, when GQDs raised to 5 mg, the specific activity was decreased which may be related to that the active site are covered by GQDs. The maximum encapsulation yields 54 % and 66 % for Cu and Co HNFs respectively were achieved when GQDs are not used during immobilization. The optimum amount of GQDs was 2.5 mg for both metal ions, specific activity of ET@GQD- Cu3(PO4)2 and ET@GQD- Cu3(PO4)2 hNFs was 473 and 220 U mg−1 respectively, which is more than free ET and ET hNFs without GQDs. This amount of GQDs (2.5 mg) was considered as the optimal value for the preparation of hNFs with other lipases. Moreover, in ET-Cu3(PO4)2 hNFs, the lipase seems to be deeply embedded inside the flower-like structure, which limited the accessibility of the active site of ET to the substrate and thus leading to low catalytic activity [53]. On the other hand, the observed enhancements in catalytic activity of some immobilized lipases can be ascribed to large surface areas in hybrid nanoflowers, which perhaps decreases the mass transfer limitations while providing conformational stability of the enzyme encapsulated in the structure of nanoflowers [57]. In other words, the nanoflower structure could offer a microenvironment for the enzyme that is favorable in certain reaction systems [14]. Furthermore, GQDs causes availability of the open form of the lipase due to improving hydrophobicity of the support, thus facilitating the availability of substrate to the active site [58].

Previous studies proved that, GO sheets, CNTs, and -Fe3O4 nanoparticles form stable flower-like structures due to oxygen containing functional groups on their surface which interact with amine groups in enzyme and also with positively charged metals [53]. The observed increase in activity could be attributed to several factors. Firstly, the strong negative charge of the carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and graphene oxide (GO) sheets may have led to the absorption of the substrate onto the nanocomposite surface. This increase in contact between the substrate and the immobilized enzyme, resulted to increase in activity [41]. By applying the optimal amount of GQDs, further investigation was performed to determine the effect of concentration on the ET on the preparation of nanoflowers. The effects of ET concentration on the encapsulation yield and activity of ET hNFs and ET@GQDs hNFs were shown in Table 2. Increasing the amount of enzyme causes a decrease in the immobilization yield [6], also with increasing the ET concentration the specific activity was increased and then decreased maybe because of mass transfer problems.

Enzyme activity is directly affected by metal and GQDs, which then affect the morphology of nanoflowers. As a result, in the presence of GQDs, the morphology of nanoflowers synthesized with copper are filled ball like structure, and with cobalt, does not have flower-like structure and is has been sheeted, which has increased the activity of the enzyme in both cases.

The activity recovery of ET hybride nanoflowers were measured and the results are shown in the Table 2. In this procedure, the activity recovery of both ET hNFs with GQDs are more than hNFs without GQDs and also activity recovery ET-QD-Cu3(PO4)2 hNFs (163 %) was more than others.

As a result, the encapsulation yield and activity of hybrid nanoflowers depends not only on the GQDs and type of metal ion, but also on the enzyme concentration. Saturation of the inorganic component (metal phosphate) at high organic concentration may have occurred, [31] leading to steric hindrance at the enzyme active [45].

The specific activity of the free TLL is around 185 U mg−1 under the assay conditions, while that of ET is almost 350 U mg−1. Previous studies have suggested that enzymes can provide multiple binding sites for metal ions which affecting the 3D structure and the activity of the immobilized enzyme [59] (Table 3 ).

By combining metal ions (Cu2+ or Co2+) with lipase and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), the formation of hybrid nanoflowers represents a promising strategy for enhancing enzyme activity and stability, outperforming both free enzyme and nanoflowers without GQDs. To confirm the stability of the hybrid nanoflowers (hNFs), a leaching test was conducted, and no protein leaching was detected, which is consistent with previous reports [45,50].

There are many literatures showed that the optimal pH of a free enzyme compared to that enzyme in immobilization form were changed [51], so the effect of pH on the activity of free lipase and hNFs are examined at pH 5.5–9.5 (Fig. 10 ). Here, the activity at pH 7.5 is set to 100 % relative to the measured activity at the various pH values. All free lipase and lipase-hNFs in this research exhibited their highest activity at pH 7.5, except ET@GQDs-Cu3(PO4)2 and TLL@GQDs-Cu3(PO4)2 which showed more activity in pH 8.5. Compared to the free enzyme, the hybrid nanoflowers (hNFs) exhibit a slightly broader pH stability profile. In particular, lipase@GQDs hNFs exhibited higher activities than free lipase and the other hNFs without GQDs (except TLL@GQDs-Co3(PO4)2 in pH 5.5 and 6.5), which indicates that GQDs act as protector for hNFs. In acidic pH, lipase-hNFs had the low catalytic activity because of degradation of hNFs in low pH against basic pH, however, for the nanoflower with GQDs, these decrease in activity are less, which is due to the protective effect of GQDs.

Thermal stability is a crucial factor for the long-term and commercial application of enzymes in chemical reactions [6] Previous research has shown that enzyme immobilization can improve thermal stability compared to the corresponding free enzyme [60]. Fig. 11 shows the effect of temperature on the activity of free lipase and lipase hybrid nanoflowers (hNFs). As the temperature increased from 30 °C, the relative activity of both free lipase and hNFs gradually decreased. However, the hNFs exhibited significantly higher activity than free lipase at all temperatures between 30 and 80 °C, indicating that the 3D structure of the nanoflowers can protect the active conformation of the enzyme and enhance its stability [61]. After a 2-h incubation at 80 °C, the free lipase lost almost all of its activity, while the lipase-hybrid nanoflowers (hNFs) retained at least 40 % of their original activity. At 80 °C, most the hNFs had lost of their activity, except ET@GQDs-Cu3(PO4)2 hNF which still retained 100 % of its original activity.

The higher pH and temperature resistance of the hybrid nanoflowers (hNFs) is thought to be attributed to the confinement of enzyme molecules within the nano-porous structures, which can increase the rigidity of the enzyme [62]. This observation is consistent with previous studies indicating that the increased stability of immobilized enzymes results from improved conformational stability of the active center of the enzyme molecule, reduced susceptibility to external environmental influences, and resistance to damage from temperature and pH [63]. Elevated temperatures can cause changes in enzyme conformation, leading to a decrease in enzyme activity [60]. Previous research has shown that enzyme stability is dependent on the degree of flexibility of its active site, which is mainly determined by interactions between the main chain and side chains [51].
Half-life of free ET and ET-hNFs at 80 °C were examined (Table 4 ). The results showed increase in half-life of all ET-hNFs (1.5–12 h) relative to free ET (50 min).

The kinetic parameters KM and Vmax are shown in Table 5 . The Km value of immobilized ET except ET-QD-Cu3(PO4)2 was increased compared to free ET (2.1 μM). An increase in Km after immobilization indicates that the immobilized enzymes have a lower affinity for their substrate compared to the free enzyme. This change may be attributed to alterations in the microenvironment of the enzyme, limitations in the mass transfer of the substrate towards active sites, and a loss of the enzyme's flexibility necessary for substrate binding. [64]. Vmax value decreased from 9.4 for free enzyme. Vmax (μmol min−1) is the maximum rate achieved by the system at maximum substrate concentrations. The lower value of Vmax for immobilized ET without QDs indicates the less interaction between of ET and p-NPB which is common in immobilization. Vmax value for ET hNFs with GQDs were increased which related to the entrapment of ET and QDs together which helped to increase of catalytic activity of lipase in open form of active site [15]. Same results have been reported in previous research whereas lipase–proline MOF exhibited higher Vmax than lipase–MOF and free lipase which reported proline effect on lipase [15].

To evaluate storage stabilities, ET-hNFs were incubated and the residual activities were assayed over 30 days (Fig. 12 ). The ET-QD-Cu3(PO4)2 hNF retained 95 % of its initial activity for 30 days of storage. Under the same conditions, other ET nanoflowers loss >5 % of their initial activity. The storage stability of the hybrid nanoflowers was again ascribed to the entrapment of the ET molecules in the nanoflower structures, providing both structural rigidity to the enzyme and a framework to prevent leakage also for the nanoflower with GQDs, the protective effect of GQDs leads to more stability.

In order to determine the reusability of ET hNFs, consecutive hydrolysis reactions of p-NPB were carried out under the reaction conditions described in Section 2.9 (Fig. 13 ). The activity of ET hNF before the first cycle was set as 100 %. After the fifth recycling, the catalyst, ET-Co3(PO4)2 hNFs, lost a maximum of 29 % of its initial activity, while ET-QD-Cu3(PO4)2 hNFs retained 88 % of its initial activity. Also, for the nanoflowers with GQDs, the decrease in activity is less, which is attributed to the protective effect of GQDs. Decreasing catalytic activity of nanoflowers could be due to physical/ structural changes in the nanoflower morphology or denaturation of enzyme.

Preparation and characterization of various lipase hybrid nanoflowers and the effect of different metal ions (Cu2+, and Co2+) and different amount of GQDs on lipase activity, stability and nanoflower morphology were investigated. These results proved that, not only the hybrid nanoflowers produced by lipase, GQDs and M2+ ions showed higher activity and stability than free enzyme, but also higher than lipase nanoflower without GQDs. Among the evaluated metal ions, hybrid nanoflowers from copper ions (Cu2+) and GQDs showed superior catalytic activity and stability, presumably as a result of high surface area of the obtained nanoflowers, an advantageous lipase conformation and microenvironment when associated with the Cu3(PO4)2 framework and also effect of GQDs on availability of the open form of the lipase and increased hydrophobicity.
We affirm that no research involving human subjects or animals was conducted in this study.
We employed established methods with detailed procedures outlined in the manuscript. Any deviations from standard methods are explained in the respective sections.
